Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to dedicate about 23 hours a week, but with limited resources at home, I had to be creative. I learn best by listening, so I'd often play lecture videos while doing other things around the house. I'd try to batch my studying around when I could access reliable internet, which wasn't always predictable.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I couldn't always stick to a set schedule because of work and family obligations that come with having limited financial resources. Some weeks I'd be super focused, other weeks I'd barely manage to log in. Deadlines were really what drove my timing more than any personal preference for routine.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I got a notification about something due, or when I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to listen to the lecture recordings when I had some quiet time. The discussion forums also pulled me in because I actually enjoyed seeing what other students were thinking. It became almost like a habit to check those posts.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, checking the discussion forums became something I just did regularly. I think it happened because I found the peer interactions genuinely interesting and it made me feel less isolated in my studies. Listening to the video content also became routine because that's how I absorb information best. The LMS became my main connection to the course since I couldn't always attend in person.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched almost 200 minutes of video content because hearing explanations really helps things click for me. The discussion forums were also valuable because reading other perspectives and posting my own thoughts helped me process the material. Those peer interactions, all 46 of them, were genuinely useful for my understanding.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete a lot of the assignments—only about 19% of them. It wasn't because I didn't think they were important, but sometimes I just ran out of time or energy. The reading materials I often skipped because reading isn't my strongest way of learning. I'd rather listen to something explained than read through dense text.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually hear someone explaining concepts clearly. If I could listen and follow along, I was in. Discussion posts felt worthwhile because they gave me a chance to engage with real people and test my understanding. Anything that felt like busy work or required resources I didn't have easy access to felt less valuable to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford to spend time on. With required ones, there was pressure to at least attempt them, even if I didn't always complete them. Optional stuff I'd only do if I was genuinely curious or had extra time, which wasn't often. The required activities created a baseline, but optional ones felt like they were designed for students with more flexibility than me.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download audio files and lecture recordings to listen to offline when my internet was unreliable. I took handwritten notes while listening because that helps me remember things better. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with family members or friends just to talk through ideas out loud.
Q10: There were many times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd replay downloaded lectures multiple times while commuting or doing chores. I'd also spend time just thinking through problems in my head, working through concepts without any digital trace. My best studying often happened away from the computer, just processing what I'd heard earlier.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy. I'd access a lecture video once, download it or take detailed notes, and then study from those materials repeatedly offline. My internet access isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed in one session. The LMS would show one view, but I might have engaged with that content five or six times offline.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My stress levels were already medium, and juggling 17 online courses meant I was constantly overwhelmed. There were weeks when I just couldn't make myself care about logging in because everything felt like too much. My attendance dropped to about 57% partly because life circumstances made it hard to show up consistently.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of zone out. Competing responsibilities, especially financial pressures from having low resources, meant coursework sometimes had to take a backseat. Confusion was also a factor—when I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes avoid it rather than face feeling lost. The workload across all my courses was genuinely overwhelming.
Q14: Oh yes, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't actually absorbed anything because I was too tired or distracted. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning. My task completion being around 42% reflects that disconnect.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. My motivation has been pretty low this semester, around a 3 out of 5, which made it hard to push through difficult tasks. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own problems. The CBT sessions I've been doing have helped me recognize this pattern, but changing it is still a work in progress.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit anything. I'd have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing to get it done. Without a deadline, I'd let things slide indefinitely. My response time averaging almost 24 hours shows I was often cutting it close. Deadlines created urgency that my low motivation couldn't generate on its own.
Q17: Feedback was actually really motivating when I got it. Seeing that I scored around 60 on assignments and 69 on exams told me I was capable of doing okay when I actually engaged. Positive feedback made me want to try harder, while critical feedback sometimes made me avoid that type of work for a while. I wish there had been more immediate feedback to keep me on track.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having 17 online courses meant I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. The flexibility of online learning was essential for my situation, but it also made it easy to fall behind. I appreciated that I could access materials on my own schedule, but I needed more structure to stay accountable.
Q19: The discussion forums were designed in a way that made engagement easier—I actually posted 19 times and had 46 peer interactions. But the assignment structure was harder for me because deadlines felt arbitrary and the workload was heavy. Having more audio-based content would have helped me a lot. The course assumed everyone had equal access to resources, which wasn't true for me.
Q20: I can totally relate to this pattern. Sometimes you're logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions, but you just can't bring yourself to complete and submit the actual assignments. It might be perfectionism, fear of failure, or just being overwhelmed by the final step. For me, the submission part felt like the hardest barrier even when I'd done the learning.
Q21: This also makes sense to me. Someone might download everything they need in a few sessions and then study intensively offline. If they're good at exams or learn efficiently through methods that don't require constant LMS presence, they could perform well. My exam score of 69 was decent even though my assignment completion was low, partly because I studied a lot offline.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't measure understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the discussions with peers outside the platform. They also can't capture motivation or the quality of engagement—whether you were really focused or just going through the motions. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and making an effort to understand it deeply. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks—it's about being mentally present and curious. For me, engagement looks like actively listening, asking questions in my head, and connecting new ideas to what I already know. It's an internal state that external metrics can't fully capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with low resources and unreliable internet access. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while working or commuting. It also wouldn't show that I was dealing with medium stress levels and working through CBT sessions to manage everything. My resilience in pushing through despite these challenges is invisible in the data.
